name: Wildform
summary: Brownish to translucent with mottling. Useful as a reference because mixed color lines often drift back toward this look.
breeding: Keep show colonies separate. In mixed-color tanks, wild-type offspring commonly reappear over generations.

name: Red Cherry
summary: The classic starter shrimp: red coloration, often with clearer patches, and very forgiving for new keepers.
breeding: The red line responds well to selection. Remove pale animals if you want the colony to become more intense.

name: Red Sakura
summary: More densely colored than basic Cherry shrimp and very common in the trade.
breeding: Sakura is a strong balance of price, color, and stability. Do not mix it with other color lines if offspring should stay red.

name: Fire Red
summary: A stronger, more opaque red. Often sold as a higher red grade than Cherry or Sakura.
breeding: Regular selection keeps the color dense. Dark substrate makes the animals look more saturated.

name: Painted Fire Red
summary: A very high red grade with strong coverage across the shell, legs, and body.
breeding: High grades benefit from strict selection. Cull translucent patches and weak leg coloration consistently.

name: Bloody Mary
summary: A deep wine-red look created by colored tissue under a more translucent shell.
breeding: Not every dark red shrimp is Bloody Mary. For true lines, check origin and offspring stability.

name: Red Rili
summary: Red head and tail sections with a pale to transparent middle body.
breeding: The Rili pattern varies. Breed from shrimp with a clear middle and clean red end sections.

name: Red Blue Rili
summary: A Rili variant with red head and tail sections plus a blue-tinted middle body.
breeding: Attractive but often variable. Best kept as its own project line instead of mixing with Blue Jelly.

name: Yellow Fire
summary: A bright yellow variety, very common in the trade and excellent for colorful species tanks.
breeding: Yellow lines look intense on dark substrate. Remove pale or greenish animals from the breeding group.

name: Yellow Neon
summary: A yellow line with a pale dorsal stripe, often sold as Goldenback or Neon.
breeding: Select for the dorsal stripe if it is part of your breeding goal. Without selection it becomes less consistent.

name: Yellow Rili
summary: A yellow Rili pattern with a transparent middle section. Less common than Red or Orange Rili.
breeding: The pattern needs selection over several generations. Stabilize it as a separate line.

name: Orange Sakura
summary: Rich orange animals, often also sold as Sunkist or Orange Fire.
breeding: Avoid mixing orange lines with red lines if you want to preserve a clean orange tone.

name: Pumpkin Orange
summary: A darker pumpkin-orange tone with strong coverage.
breeding: Watch for even color. Separate animals that are too yellow or too red if you want a tight color range.

name: Orange Rili
summary: Orange head and tail sections with a clear middle body. A common Rili strain for colorful aquascapes.
breeding: A clean split between orange and transparent areas is the goal. Partly solid animals weaken the pattern.

name: Green Jade
summary: Ranges from olive to emerald green. Very popular, but often less uniform than red, yellow, or blue lines.
breeding: Green can vary strongly in quality and offspring. Good starter stock and consistent selection are worth it.

name: Green Rili
summary: A green Rili variant, much rarer and usually more project-like than Red or Orange Rili.
breeding: Stabilize it patiently. Offspring can vary widely between green, clear, and wild-type looks.

name: Blue Dream
summary: A strong blue variety and one of the most popular modern Neocaridina strains.
breeding: Do not casually cross Blue Dream with Blue Velvet or Blue Jelly. Trade names are sometimes used loosely.

name: Blue Velvet
summary: A light to medium blue strain with a clean, bright aquarium presence.
breeding: Blue Velvet and Blue Dream can be different lines depending on origin. Keep them separate for stable color.

name: Blue Jelly
summary: A translucent pale blue, often connected to Rili-related lines.
breeding: Reddish markings can appear. Breed only suitable animals if you want a clear Jelly blue.

name: Blue Diamond
summary: A dark, deep-blue appearance, often associated with black/blue lineages.
breeding: Select for even dark coverage and separate very pale offspring.

name: Blue Carbon Rili
summary: Dark head and tail sections with a blue or transparent body area.
breeding: The balance of black, blue, and clear areas varies. Good line management matters more than the name.

name: OE Blue Dream
summary: Blue Dream animals with orange eyes. Eye-catching, but less common than standard Blue Dream.
breeding: Maintain Orange Eyes separately. When buying, check whether eye color and body color breed true.

name: Black Rose
summary: Very dark to black Neocaridina, popular in bright planted tanks.
breeding: Brownish or bluish offspring can appear. Select for dense shell color if you want a rich black colony.

name: Black Sakura
summary: A dark Sakura/Fire-style interpretation with high coverage, often close to Black Rose depending on seller.
breeding: Names overlap. Judge the line rather than the trade label and remove pale animals.

name: Chocolate
summary: A warm brown to chocolate-colored variety. Less flashy, but excellent in natural-style layouts.
breeding: Do not confuse it with wild type. Good Chocolate lines are clearly more even and richer brown.

name: Snowball / White Pearl
summary: White to milky-translucent Neocaridina. Popular in the hobby, though not always treated cleanly as davidi in taxonomy.
breeding: For strict davidi collections, verify origin. In practice it is often kept alongside davidi color strains as a Neo variety.

name: Crystal Red B
summary: Entry-level Crystal Red with sparse red banding over a white body. A good introduction to Caridina keeping.
breeding: Soft, acidic water is essential. Crystal shrimp will not thrive or breed in hard water. Use RO water buffered with active substrate.

name: Crystal Red S
summary: Balanced red and white banding, the most common grade in the trade. Good starter grade for Crystal Red.
breeding: Select for clean, crisp band edges. Fuzzy or uneven transitions lower the grade of offspring over time.

name: Crystal Red SS
summary: More intense red than S grade. White areas remain bright and well-defined.
breeding: Grade stability improves with strict culling. Remove animals with soft band edges or excessive white.

name: Crystal Red SSS
summary: Near-perfect red saturation with minimal white. The top standard grade before specialty variants.
breeding: SSS to SSS pairings do not reliably produce SSS offspring. Expect a spread of grades; only the top fraction qualifies.

name: Crystal Red C
summary: Hinomaru and related extreme grades with maximum red coverage. Often nearly fully red with a white patch on the head only.
breeding: True C-grade animals are rare even from top breeders. Water quality deviations will cause moulting failures and deaths.

name: Crystal Black S
summary: The black counterpart to Crystal Red, with high-contrast black and white banding.
breeding: Same water requirements as Crystal Red. Black and Red can produce a mix of both in offspring — keep lines separate for purity.

name: Blue Bolt
summary: Vivid blue body with white banding, one of the most sought-after Taiwan Bee variants. Blue Bolt originate from crossing Crystal with other Caridina.
breeding: Crossing with other Taiwan Bee variants is common. Keep pairings intentional — mixed offspring lose the clean blue.

name: Panda
summary: Black and white banding in a distinctive panda pattern. One of the foundational Taiwan Bee forms.
breeding: Panda x Panda gives the most consistent panda offspring. Crossing with Blue Bolt shifts the black toward blue in some offspring.

name: Shadow Panda
summary: A darker, cooler variation on the Panda — the black takes on a blue-charcoal tone against pale-blue white areas.
breeding: Shadow coloration is influenced by both genetics and light spectrum. Consistent blue-tinted lighting helps express the shade.

name: King Kong
summary: Nearly fully black body with only residual white markings. One of the darkest Taiwan Bee expressions.
breeding: The extreme dark coverage makes grade assessment difficult in juveniles. Evaluate adults only.

name: Wine Red
summary: A deep burgundy to dark wine-red Taiwan Bee. The distinctive color comes from selective breeding within the Taiwan Bee complex.
breeding: Wine Red x Wine Red gives the highest chance of consistent offspring. Crossing with Panda or Blue Bolt shifts color significantly.

name: Snow White
summary: A white-to-pale-blue Taiwan Bee with a clean, translucent shell. Often called White Bee or Snow Bee.
breeding: Snow White crossed with Blue Bolt can produce pale-blue offspring that fall between both forms — useful in colour projects.

name: Orange-Eye Tiger (OBT)
summary: Orange body with dark vertical stripes and distinctive orange eyes. OBT is also used in Taiwan Bee crossing to add vigour.
breeding: OBT is hardier than Crystal or Taiwan Bee variants. It tolerates slightly harder water but still benefits from soft, acidic conditions.

name: Red Tiger
summary: Red base colour with dark striping. Rarer than OBT and often more sensitive to water quality swings.
breeding: Keep with other Tiger variants or pure. Crossing with OBT is possible but shifts the red toward orange in offspring.

name: Black Tiger
summary: Near-black body with subtle darker striping. The darkest of the Tiger forms.
breeding: Black Tiger selection is straightforward: remove animals that show significant orange or grey areas.

name: Fancy Tiger
summary: A mixed-expression Tiger showing elements of both red and orange in overlapping or gradient banding. Results from crossing different Tiger lines.
breeding: Breeding Fancy Tiger is unpredictable by definition. Offspring vary widely — stabilising a specific pattern requires dedicated selection.

name: Cardinal Shrimp
summary: Vivid scarlet body with brilliant white spots. Endemic to Lake Matano, Sulawesi. One of the most visually striking freshwater shrimp in the hobby.
breeding: Requires very specific warm, alkaline water (pH 7.8–8.5, 28–30 °C) — opposite of other soft-water Caridina. Cannot be kept with Crystal or Taiwan Bee.

name: White Glove Shrimp
summary: Dark brown to black body with striking white legs — giving the appearance of wearing gloves. Endemic to Sulawesi lakes.
breeding: Same Sulawesi parameters as Cardinal shrimp. The white leg coloration is genetically stable but can fade under poor water quality.

name: White Orchid Shrimp
summary: Creamy white to pale tan with a soft mottled pattern. Less vivid than Cardinal but highly elegant.
breeding: Sulawesi parameters required. Sensitive to fluctuations — a stable setup matters more than exact values at the extremes.

name: Blue Leg Poso
summary: Dark translucent body with vivid blue legs. Endemic to Lake Poso, Sulawesi. Smaller and rarer than Cardinal.
breeding: Lake Poso water is warmer and slightly different from Matano — replicate accordingly. Not easily mixed with Cardinal in the same tank.

name: Amano Shrimp
summary: Olive-translucent with a row of dashes along the lateral line. The most effective algae-eating shrimp in the hobby, popularised by Takashi Amano.
breeding: Requires brackish water for larvae to develop — nearly impossible to breed in a standard freshwater tank. Wild-caught or hatchery stock only.

name: Bamboo / Wood Shrimp
summary: Large earthy-brown filter-feeder that fans fine particles from the water column. Dramatic posture and behaviour make it a display centrepiece.
breeding: Larvae require brackish conditions and cannot be raised in freshwater. In a mature tank they pose no threat to other shrimp.

name: Vampire / Viper Shrimp
summary: West African filter-feeder with a dusty blue-grey body. Uses fan-like appendages to catch microparticles, similar to Bamboo shrimp but from a different continent.
breeding: Same constraints as Bamboo shrimp — brackish larvae. Rarely bred in captivity. Best kept in well-established, high-flow tanks.
